original_abstract: Soil pH is traditionally regarded as the "master variable" governing nutrient availability, yet conventional models often prioritize soil chemical reactions while neglecting the physiological role of plant roots. This review argues that nutrient bioavailability is determined by a complex interplay between soil sorption processes and plant uptake kinetics, both of which are influenced by pH-dependent variable charge surfaces. By synthesizing data across various ions, we demonstrate that plant and soil responses to pH frequently act in opposing directions. For instance, while increasing pH reduces sulfate and phosphate sorption by soil (theoretically increasing availability), it simultaneously inhibits the rate of uptake by plant roots. In the case of phosphate, the plant physiological effect is dominant, leading to decreased uptake at higher pH levels despite lower soil retention. Conversely, for molybdate, the dramatic reduction in soil adsorption at high pH outweighs plant inhibitory effects, resulting in greater overall availability. Cation availability (e.g., zinc and copper) shows a more balanced interaction, as both soil sorption and plant uptake rates increase with pH, though the net effect often trends toward a slight decrease in availability. Unique cases like boron highlight that uncharged molecules are governed by different principles, with availability tied directly to the concentration of boric acid. We conclude that relying solely on soil-centric diagrams provides a distorted view of nutrient dynamics and emphasize that accurate assessments of fertility must integrate both soil chemistry and plant physiology.
humanized_abstract: Soil pH is generally regarded as the key master variable controlling nutrient availability in soils. Present models of nutrient availability are predominantly based on soil chemical models with a limited consideration of the role of the root plant. We present evidence of a coupled control of nutrient soil sorption and root plant uptake bioavailability by pH, through a variable charge surface dependency. Data from several studies for a number of essential plant nutrients show a coupled response between plant and soil and which, for several nutrients are opposite to expectation from current models of nutrient availability. Increases in soil pH are accompanied by decreases in sulphate and phosphate adsorption by the soil; yet simultaneously decreases plant root uptake of these ions. A dominant plant effect of pH on the uptake of phosphate occurs, making nutrient availability to the plant lower in high pH soils in spite of the decrease in soil adsorption for these ions. Conversely, increases in the case of molybdate, soil adsorption is low at high pH and, while some inhibitory root effect may occur, the magnitude of soil adsorption is so reduced that the nutrient is more available in higher pH soils. The effect of pH on availability of essential cations such as zinc and copper follows a more balanced response where, while soil adsorption increases with increasing pH, root plant uptake is enhanced too, with availability of these nutrients decreasing only marginally in higher pH soils. Models of nutrient soil bioavailability which account for plant effects in addition to the soil adsorption properties allow an improved description of soil fertility. Current soil chemical models do not describe the nutrient availability properly, as a complete picture is given only when one accounts for both the soil chemistry and the root plant physiology. Such a description considers in particular the charge of the ions involved in soil fertility, like in the case of boron nutrient availability, which is solely given by the concentration of the boric acid species.
